author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: I do find this very cool. Tho, the internal friction seems to be fairly high based on the effort needed to turn the device in the video. I am not sure I understand the alternating placement of the bearings -- do both bearings touch the turning barrel?  (Because one of the bearings in the alternating pattern looks like it is the flat side, and I don't see how that would work efficiently).

replies.author: @LawrenceMarks27
replies.text: He has a video specifically on the bearings that will help you to understand that better.  I printed one and it actually moves fairly smooth.  I was going to ask a similar question though.  If it's a matter of friction making it so tough to turn, or if it just requires that much torque? If its friction though, I would imagine you can clean some of that up by playing around with tolerances on the pieces to be a little less tight of a fit for more free range motion.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the closeness of the yellow rollers to the 'ground ring' did make it hard to turn and it does need 'mucking around with' to get that better but that's all there was to it really and you make a good point it might be better to replace that section - the 'wolfrom section, with gears - nice thinking mate thanks for sharing - i think it will improve the overall machine if i did exactly that - the bearings are cross roller bearings and i have done a video on them and how they work you might want to check that out

Thanks Rob!  Only limited by your imagination is a great way to begin with a project.

author: @jtraveler888
text:
💯 Very interesting. big Like! 👍
I have long been fascinated with efficient and elegant mechanisms.
Your channel has some great content and I have 3D printed several of your designs.
The Wolfrom drive I knew as the radar bearing or split annulus differential planetary gear for high ratios in small spaces.
I'm also interested in high force rotary to linear actuators such as the differential planetary roller screw. These are often inverted.
I would enjoy seeing your coverage, explanation, and design of Differential Planetary Roller Screws.
Thank you.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That's a nice suggestion mate thanks for taking the time to say it and I am glad you like the video

author: @Rebar77_real
text:
I wonder how well a coil or two placed lengthwise in the main cylinder(s) would do attached to your meter! Thinking if you have many magnets in the rollers and while they spin they could generate power? Or even capture just enough residual energy to counter normal transmission loss from a windfoil, make that moot, type thing. 

Fun to think about! Cheers all!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting mate - definitely fun thinking

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
A marvelous winch you have there!  Very nice.  I think the diameter of the inner rollers doesn't matter so much to the drive ratio as the length of the belts. Even though the inner scrolls appear to be part of the driving train, they are effectively idlers.  The drive to the outside drum is via the belt.  The two ratios to look at will be the size of the inner drum to the length of the belt, and the belt length to the inner side of the drum.
Possibly the diameter of the inner drum to the diameter of the scrollers+drum might be a better way to calculte the final drive ratio.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate they aren't idle - the belt doesn't touch the outer drive drum as the belts are sunk into the rollers so the belts don't actually touch the output drive cylinder. The raised bumps on the yellow rollers are the part that transfers torque

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, you're right.  My fault.  I didn't really think it through.  Still trying to figure out the ratio.  Thanks.
